content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Consolidation with mitigation (closure as default, compensatory measures)'. Closing small-village primary schools in depopulating Hungarian settlements is not justified as an efficiency measure on current evidence, because per-pupil cost premiums are economically defensible, learning outcomes do not suffer materially, and closure-induced local population loss and community harm systematically outweigh accounting savings.
opportunities: Enables investment concentration in fewer, better-equipped consolidated sites, following the Portuguese centros escolares pattern of matched receiving-site investment.
Statutory transport guarantees and targeted support provide a formal, legally enforceable floor of protection for displaced pupils, an improvement over ad hoc arrangements.
strengths: Hungary's primary education school-site network declined from 3,609 sites (2021) to 3,521 sites (2024), while over 1,200 of 3,155 settlements now have no public educational institution.
Hungary's annual births fell 22.5% in ten years (110,800 in 2015 to 77,600 in 2025), with primary-grade enrollment projected to shrink from ~110,000 to ~72,000 annually by 2030.
threats: Compensatory measures (transport guarantees, TEIP-style support) require sustained multi-year funding; without a specified binding funding-commitment mechanism (e.g., ring-fenced budget line, multi-year statutory allocation), these measures risk being cut in future budget cycles once the initial closure has already occurred and political attention has moved on.

Closure decisions, even with consultation, may trigger community mobilization (as in the 2007 Erdőkürt 'Human Chain for Rural Schools' precedent), and limited judicial review (legality-only, not policy-merit) means affected communities have few formal channels to contest closure substance.
weaknesses: The magnitude and timeline of actual primary-grade enrollment loss in rural versus urban areas remain uncertain; district-level projections are scarce.

The actual cost differential between small-village school operation and consolidated/bussed alternatives (including all transportation, facilities, teacher deployment, and psychosocial costs) has not been reliably estimated for Hungary.
